sources.title: Responsible Lending Notification (SorKorChor. 7/2566)
sources.summary: BOT's core responsible lending framework requiring banks to conduct proper credit assessments based on borrower's repayment ability. Updated January 2025 with stricter DSR (Debt Service Ratio) guidelines and Risk-Based Pricing requirements.
key_points: Mandates ability-to-repay assessments for all retail loans
Introduces DSR-based macroprudential limits
Requires transparent pricing and fair credit criteria
Effective for all supervised financial institutions
sources.compliance_notes: Primary Thai regulatory anchor for any credit scoring system deployed in Thailand. Non-compliance carries supervisory sanctions.
sources.citation: Bank of Thailand. (2023, updated 2025). Responsible Lending Notification (SorKorChor. 7/2566). Bank of Thailand.

sources.title: Debt Service Ratio (DSR) Macroprudential Policy
sources.summary: BOT macroprudential policy setting maximum Debt Service Ratio thresholds for different loan types to prevent household debt over-leveraging. Core input to credit underwriting models.
key_points: DSR cap = total monthly debt obligations / monthly gross income
Differentiated limits by loan type (mortgage, personal, auto)
Systemic risk tool to prevent household debt buildup
Lenders must verify income and existing obligations
sources.compliance_notes: DSR computation must be embedded in credit scoring pipeline as a hard constraint, not just a model feature.
sources.citation: Bank of Thailand. (2023). Debt Service Ratio (DSR) Macroprudential Policy. Bank of Thailand.

sources.title: Risk-Based Pricing (RBP) Regulatory Sandbox
sources.summary: BOT regulatory sandbox framework allowing financial institutions to pilot risk-based pricing models where interest rates are differentiated by individual credit risk scores, instead of uniform pricing.
key_points: Allows rate differentiation based on model-predicted risk
Requires model transparency and fairness demonstration
Sandbox participation requires BOT approval
Links credit score output directly to pricing decisions
sources.compliance_notes: AI credit scoring that feeds into pricing decisions may be subject to RBP sandbox requirements.
sources.citation: Bank of Thailand. (2022, updated 2024). Risk-Based Pricing Regulatory Sandbox. Bank of Thailand.

sources.title: National Credit Bureau (NCB) Thailand — Credit Score Overview
sources.summary: Thailand's official credit bureau providing credit reports and scores for individuals and SMEs. NCB scores are the primary traditional credit signal used by Thai financial institutions.
key_points: NCB score range: 300–900
Key score factors: payment history, credit utilization, credit history length, credit mix, new inquiries
Coverage: banks, finance companies, credit card issuers
Thin-file problem: many Thais lack NCB history — opportunity for alternative data
sources.compliance_notes: Any model using NCB data must comply with PDPA and NCB member data-sharing agreements.
sources.citation: National Credit Bureau. (2024). NCB Credit Score Overview. National Credit Bureau of Thailand.

sources.title: Personal Data Protection Act B.E. 2562 (PDPA) — Credit Scoring Implications
sources.organization: Office of the Personal Data Protection Committee (PDPC)
sources.summary: Thailand's primary data protection law, equivalent to GDPR in scope. Governs collection, use, and processing of personal data. Credit scoring models using personal data must comply with PDPA lawful basis requirements, consent, and data subject rights.
key_points: Requires lawful basis for processing personal data (consent, contract, legitimate interest, legal obligation)
Data subjects have right to access, rectify, erase their data
Automated decision-making provisions similar to GDPR Article 22
Cross-border transfer restrictions
Sensitive data (financial, health) requires explicit consent
sources.compliance_notes: Credit scoring models must document lawful basis. Automated rejection decisions must allow human review if requested. PDPA applies to all Thai-market deployments.
sources.citation: Office of the Personal Data Protection Committee. (2022). Personal Data Protection Act B.E. 2562. Royal Gazette.

sources.title: SME Credit Risk Database and NaCGA Credit Guarantee Scheme
sources.organization: Bank of Thailand / National Credit Guarantee Agency (NaCGA)
sources.summary: BOT's SME credit risk database initiative and the NaCGA credit guarantee scheme that enables credit access for SMEs lacking collateral. Relevant for designing SME-specific credit scoring models.
key_points: SME Credit Risk Database aggregates financial data for credit assessment
NaCGA provides partial guarantees to reduce lender risk on SME loans
Alternative financial indicators used: revenue, receivables, inventory
Policy aims to reduce credit gap for Thai SMEs
sources.compliance_notes: SME credit scoring models must account for NaCGA guarantee interactions and BOT SME reporting requirements.
sources.citation: Bank of Thailand. (2024). SME Credit Risk Database. Bank of Thailand.

sources.title: ADB Working Paper: Access to Finance for SMEs in Thailand
sources.summary: ADB working paper analyzing barriers to SME financing in Thailand, including credit assessment challenges, data gaps, and policy recommendations for improving SME credit access through better risk evaluation tools.
key_points: Thai SMEs face collateral constraints and lack of credit history
Cash flow-based lending models outperform collateral-based for SMEs
Alternative data (tax records, utility, e-commerce) can bridge data gaps
Policy recommendations include public credit guarantee expansion
sources.citation: Asian Development Bank. (2021). Access to Finance for SMEs in Thailand. ADB Working Paper.

sources.title: FICO Score — Understanding Your Credit Score (5 Factor Model)
sources.summary: FICO's official explanation of the 5-factor FICO score model, the global industry standard for consumer credit scoring. Provides foundational methodology that most scorecard models are benchmarked against.
key_points: Payment History: 35% (most important — on-time payments)
Amounts Owed / Credit Utilization: 30%
Length of Credit History: 15%
Credit Mix: 10% (variety of account types)
New Credit / Hard Inquiries: 10%
Score range: 300–850
sources.compliance_notes: FICO factors are a useful baseline for feature engineering in custom scorecards. Direct FICO score use in Thailand requires licensing.
sources.citation: Fair Isaac Corporation. (2024). What's in Your Credit Score? myFICO.

sources.title: Weight of Evidence (WoE) and Information Value (IV) for Logistic Regression Scorecards
sources.summary: Comprehensive technical guide on WoE/IV methodology for traditional logistic regression scorecard development. Covers binning strategy, WoE calculation, IV thresholds for variable selection, and scorecard scaling.
key_points: WoE = ln(Distribution of Events / Distribution of Non-Events)
IV < 0.02: useless; 0.02–0.1: weak; 0.1–0.3: medium; >0.3: strong predictor
WoE transformation improves logistic regression with non-linear patterns
Scorecard points = offset + factor × ln(odds)
Standard in traditional bank credit modeling (Basel IRB)
sources.compliance_notes: WoE/LR scorecards are the most regulatory-accepted approach (full interpretability). Preferred for Basel IRB PD model documentation.
sources.citation: Bhalla, D. (2023). Weight of Evidence (WoE) and Information Value (IV). listendata.com.

sources.title: Credit Risk Scorecard Development Using WoE and Logistic Regression
sources.summary: Practical guide to building credit risk scorecards using WoE transformation and logistic regression. Covers end-to-end development pipeline from data prep through model validation and scorecard calibration.
key_points: End-to-end scorecard development workflow
Binning optimization (coarse vs fine classing)
Gini / KS / AUC validation metrics
Population Stability Index (PSI) for monitoring
Scorecard to points conversion methodology
sources.citation: Altair. (2022). Credit Risk Scorecard Development. Altair.

sources.title: Machine Learning Methods for Credit Risk: Comparative Study of XGBoost, LightGBM, Random Forest
sources.summary: Peer-reviewed comparative study of ML algorithms (XGBoost, LightGBM, Random Forest, Logistic Regression) applied to credit default prediction. Benchmarks performance and discusses practical deployment considerations.
key_points: XGBoost and LightGBM consistently outperform traditional logistic regression on AUC
Gradient boosting handles imbalanced classes better than linear models
Feature importance rankings show payment behavior and utilization as top predictors
Model stacking (ensemble) provides marginal gains with complexity trade-off
Interpretability gap remains a deployment challenge for regulatory contexts
sources.compliance_notes: ML models must be paired with XAI (SHAP/LIME) for regulatory acceptance. Pure black-box ML is not acceptable under BOT guidelines.
sources.citation: Author et al. (2023). Machine Learning Methods for Credit Risk. PLOS ONE.

sources.title: Deep Learning and Ensemble Methods for Credit Scoring: Systematic Review
sources.summary: 2024 systematic review of deep learning (LSTM, Transformer, neural networks) and ensemble approaches for credit scoring. Covers recent advances in handling sequential data and alternative data fusion.
key_points: LSTM handles sequential repayment behavior better than static features
Transformer-based models show promise for behavioral data
Graph Neural Networks useful for relationship-based credit (peer lending, SME)
Tabular data: tree-based ensembles still competitive with deep learning
Data augmentation techniques for class imbalance (SMOTE, ADASYN)
sources.compliance_notes: Deep learning models face highest regulatory scrutiny; require robust XAI layer.
sources.citation: Author et al. (2024). Deep Learning for Credit Scoring. Nature Scientific Reports.

sources.title: Alternative Data for Credit Risk Assessment: World Bank Report
sources.summary: World Bank / CGAP report examining the use of alternative data (telco, utility, e-commerce, psychometric, social media) for credit scoring thin-file and unbanked populations. Covers data quality, regulatory considerations, and implementation case studies.
key_points: Alternative data types: telecom airtime usage, utility payments, rental history, social media, psychometric tests, e-commerce transaction data
Most predictive: telecom data, utility payments, e-commerce (proven lift in AUC)
Social media / psychometric: higher bias risk, regulatory caution warranted
Key challenge: data access agreements and privacy compliance
Case studies from Africa, Southeast Asia, Latin America
sources.compliance_notes: Alternative data use in Thailand must comply with PDPA. Consent required for non-traditional data sources. Telecom data sharing requires carrier agreements.
sources.citation: World Bank / CGAP. (2022). Alternative Data for Credit Risk Assessment. World Bank.

sources.title: Big Data and Machine Learning for Credit Access and Financial Inclusion
sources.summary: SSRN working paper examining how big data and ML models using alternative data sources can expand credit access, particularly for underserved populations. Empirical evidence from FinTech lenders.
key_points: ML models with alternative data approve 30–40% more borrowers with same or better default rates
Digital footprint data (browsing, app usage) predictive but raises privacy concerns
FinTech lenders demonstrably outperform banks in thin-file markets using alternative data
Risk of disparate impact: alternative data can proxy for protected characteristics
sources.compliance_notes: Disparate impact analysis essential when using digital footprint or behavioral data.
sources.citation: Author et al. (2020). Big Data and ML for Financial Inclusion. SSRN Working Paper.

sources.title: AFI Report: Digital Financial Services and Alternative Credit Scoring for Financial Inclusion
sources.summary: AFI policy report on using digital financial services data (mobile money, agent banking, digital payments) for alternative credit scoring to promote financial inclusion across developing economies, with Southeast Asia case studies.
key_points: Mobile money transaction history strongly predictive of creditworthiness
Agent banking data can substitute for traditional bank data in rural areas
Regulatory sandboxes key for testing alternative scoring models
Consumer protection considerations: transparency, redress mechanisms
Thailand, Philippines, Indonesia highlighted as active markets
sources.compliance_notes: Relevant for Thai rural and low-income segment credit access. Mobile wallet (PromptPay, TrueMoney) data as potential alternative data source.
sources.citation: Alliance for Financial Inclusion. (2020). Digital Financial Services and Alternative Credit Scoring. AFI.

sources.title: SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) — Original Paper and Documentation
sources.summary: SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) is the leading XAI method for explaining ML model predictions. Based on cooperative game theory Shapley values, it provides consistent, locally accurate feature attribution for any ML model.
key_points: SHAP values: fair attribution of prediction contribution to each feature
Works with any model: tree SHAP (exact, fast for XGBoost/LightGBM), kernel SHAP (model-agnostic)
Produces: force plots, summary plots, dependency plots, waterfall plots
Enables adverse action explanations: top reason codes for credit denials
SHAP interaction values capture feature interactions
Used by US/EU regulators as acceptable XAI method for credit scoring
sources.compliance_notes: SHAP is the recommended XAI method for credit scoring adverse action explanations. Satisfies ECOA/Regulation B reason code requirements and aligns with EU AI Act explainability requirements.
sources.citation: Lundberg, S. M., & Lee, S. I. (2017). A Unified Approach to Interpreting Model Predictions. NeurIPS.

sources.title: LIME (Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations)
sources.summary: LIME generates local surrogate linear models to explain individual predictions from any black-box model. A widely used XAI baseline, though superseded by SHAP in most credit scoring applications due to stability and consistency issues.
key_points: Locally approximates complex model behavior with interpretable linear model
Model-agnostic: works with any classifier
Less stable than SHAP (perturbed samples vary between runs)
Simpler to implement but less theoretically grounded than SHAP
Useful as secondary explanation method for validation / comparison
sources.compliance_notes: LIME is acceptable but SHAP preferred due to consistency guarantee. Use LIME as a secondary validation tool.
sources.citation: Ribeiro, M. T., Singh, S., & Guestrin, C. (2016). 'Why Should I Trust You?': Explaining the Predictions of Any Classifier. KDD 2016.

sources.title: Explainable AI in Credit Scoring: Regulatory Requirements and Implementation
sources.summary: CFA Institute report on XAI requirements and practices in financial services, covering regulatory expectations, practical implementation approaches, and governance frameworks for explainable AI in credit decisions.
key_points: Regulatory expectation: models must be auditable, outcomes explainable to applicants
Minimum: top 3–5 reason codes per credit decision
Intrinsically interpretable models (scorecard, decision tree) vs post-hoc XAI (SHAP)
Model governance: version control, validation, ongoing monitoring
Human-in-the-loop requirements for high-stakes decisions
sources.compliance_notes: Relevant for BOT credit model governance requirements and any EU AI Act compliance.
sources.citation: CFA Institute. (2023). Explainable AI in Finance. CFA Institute Research.

sources.title: CFPB Guidance on AI and Machine Learning in Credit Decisions — Adverse Action Notices
sources.summary: CFPB guidance clarifying that adverse action notice requirements (ECOA / Regulation B) apply equally to complex AI/ML models as to traditional scorecards. Lenders cannot use 'complex model' as justification for vague reason codes.
key_points: ECOA requires specific, accurate reason codes for any credit denial
Complex AI models must still provide principal reason statements
SHAP-derived reason codes are acceptable if accurately reflecting model behavior
'The model decided' is NOT an acceptable adverse action reason
Applies to any model used in credit decisions for US consumers
sources.compliance_notes: While US-specific, this guidance is highly influential globally. BOT's consumer protection framework has analogous adverse action requirements. Any Thai credit AI should adopt this standard as best practice.
sources.citation: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau. (2022). CFPB Acts to Protect People from Illegal Credit Denials Caused by Complex Algorithms. CFPB.

sources.title: CFPB Fair Lending Annual Report 2023 — AI/ML Examination Focus
sources.summary: CFPB's 2023 fair lending examination report, with dedicated sections on AI/ML model bias, Less Discriminatory Alternative (LDA) analysis requirements, and enforcement cases involving algorithmic discrimination.
key_points: Regulators now require proactive testing for Less Discriminatory Alternatives (LDA)
Disparate impact analysis: if proxy variables correlate with protected class → scrutiny
CFPB examines model development process, not just outcomes
Adverse action reason code accuracy is an examination focus
Enforcement actions brought against lenders using biased ML models
sources.compliance_notes: LDA analysis is becoming a global best practice. Thai AI credit models should document LDA testing even without formal US regulatory requirement.
sources.citation: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau. (2024). Fair Lending Report of the CFPB 2023. CFPB.

sources.title: EU AI Act — Annex III High-Risk AI Systems: Credit Scoring Classification
sources.summary: EU AI Act Annex III classifies AI systems used for creditworthiness assessment and credit scoring as high-risk AI systems, triggering comprehensive compliance requirements for transparency, human oversight, data governance, and technical documentation.
key_points: Credit scoring explicitly listed as High-Risk AI (Annex III, point 5b)
Requirements: conformity assessment, technical documentation, transparency measures
Fundamental rights impact assessment required
Human oversight provisions: meaningful human review of decisions
Prohibited: subliminal manipulation, real-time biometric scoring
Effective for high-risk systems: August 2, 2026
Extraterritorial reach: applies if EU persons affected
sources.compliance_notes: Thai financial institutions with EU customers or investors must plan for EU AI Act compliance by August 2026. Even without EU exposure, Annex III requirements set the global gold standard for responsible AI credit scoring.
sources.citation: European Parliament. (2024). Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (EU AI Act), Annex III. Official Journal of the European Union.

sources.title: Basel III Internal Ratings-Based (IRB) Approach — Credit Risk Capital Requirements
sources.summary: BIS Basel framework for internal credit risk models (IRB approach). Defines PD (Probability of Default), LGD (Loss Given Default), and EAD (Exposure at Default) as the three core model components for credit risk capital calculation.
key_points: PD (Probability of Default): 1-year probability borrower defaults
LGD (Loss Given Default): % of exposure lost if default occurs
EAD (Exposure at Default): outstanding amount at time of default
Expected Loss = PD × LGD × EAD
Foundation IRB: supervisor provides LGD/EAD; Advanced IRB: bank estimates all
Model validation requirements: backtesting, benchmarking, use test
Conservative adjustments required for model uncertainty
sources.compliance_notes: BOT-supervised banks using IRB approach must comply with BOT's Basel implementation guidelines. PD models are directly equivalent to credit scoring models.
sources.citation: Basel Committee on Banking Supervision. (2006, updated 2023). Basel III: A Global Regulatory Framework. BIS.

sources.title: World Bank Global Findex Database 2021 — Financial Inclusion Data
sources.summary: World Bank's flagship financial inclusion database covering 148 countries. Provides data on account ownership, credit access, savings behavior, and digital payment adoption — including Thailand. Critical for understanding baseline financial inclusion levels.
key_points: Global: 76% of adults have bank accounts (2021 vs 51% in 2011)
Thailand: ~95% bank account ownership — relatively high financial inclusion
But formal credit access remains lower than account ownership
Mobile money adoption growing rapidly in Southeast Asia
Gender gap in credit access persists in many markets
sources.citation: World Bank. (2022). The Global Findex Database 2021. World Bank Group.

sources.title: IFC Credit Infrastructure and Responsible Finance — SME Credit Access
sources.summary: IFC framework for responsible credit infrastructure development including credit bureaus, collateral registries, and insolvency frameworks. Provides guidance on enabling credit access especially for SMEs and underserved segments.
key_points: Credit infrastructure pillars: credit bureau, collateral registry, insolvency framework
SME credit gap in developing countries estimated at USD 5.2 trillion
Credit bureau coverage expansion key to reducing information asymmetry
Alternative credit infrastructure: movable asset registries for SME collateral
IFC supports NCB development in emerging markets
sources.citation: International Finance Corporation. (2023). Credit Infrastructure. IFC World Bank Group.

sources.title: A Comparative Study of ML Algorithms for Credit Default Prediction (arXiv)
sources.summary: arXiv preprint comparing classical and modern ML approaches for credit default prediction across multiple datasets. Evaluates precision-recall trade-offs, class imbalance handling, and feature importance consistency.
key_points: CatBoost competitive with XGBoost for categorical-heavy credit data
Class imbalance handling: SMOTE + undersampling outperforms SMOTE alone
AUC-ROC vs AUC-PR: PR curve more informative for imbalanced credit data
Calibration critical: probability outputs must be well-calibrated for PD models
Isotonic regression and Platt scaling for probability calibration
sources.compliance_notes: Model calibration (not just discrimination) is required for PD estimation under Basel IRB.
sources.citation: Author et al. (2023). Comparative Study of ML for Credit Default. arXiv preprint.

sources.title: ASEAN SME Financing Benchmark and Digital Lending Landscape
sources.summary: ASEAN-wide benchmark report on SME financing conditions, digital lending models, and alternative credit scoring approaches across member states. Includes Thailand-specific data on SME credit penetration and FinTech lending.
key_points: Thailand SME credit penetration ~40% of GDP — moderate vs regional peers
Digital lending (P2P, FinTech) growing but not yet systemically significant
Invoice financing and supply chain finance most promising SME alt-lending products
Data sharing between tax authority and banks would unlock significant scoring improvements
Regulatory harmonization needed for cross-border SME lending
sources.citation: ASEAN Secretariat / ADB. (2022). ASEAN SME Financing Benchmark. ADB.

sources.title: Fairness in Machine Learning — Survey and Taxonomy
sources.organization: Springer / Journal of Machine Learning Research
sources.summary: Comprehensive JMLR survey of ML fairness definitions, metrics, and mitigation techniques. Provides the theoretical foundation for bias measurement and fairness-aware model training in credit and other high-stakes settings.
key_points: Fairness definitions: demographic parity, equalized odds, individual fairness, counterfactual fairness
Impossibility theorem: cannot simultaneously satisfy all fairness criteria
Bias sources: historical bias, representation bias, measurement bias, algorithmic bias
Mitigation stages: pre-processing (resampling), in-processing (constrained learning), post-processing (threshold adjustment)
Fairness-accuracy trade-off: reducing bias typically reduces aggregate performance
sources.compliance_notes: Understanding fairness taxonomy essential for documenting compliance with ECOA/EU AI Act non-discrimination requirements.
sources.citation: Mehrabi, N. et al. (2019). A Survey on Bias and Fairness in Machine Learning. JMLR / arXiv.

sources.title: Logistic Regression and WoE Scorecard: Credit Risk Modeling Best Practices
sources.summary: Academic paper formalizing WoE/IV scorecard methodology with focus on credit risk modeling. Covers theoretical basis, development best practices, validation methods, and comparison with ML approaches.
key_points: Formal statistical basis for WoE transformation
IV as a variable selection criterion with theoretical justification
Scorecard calibration and odds-to-score conversion
Validation: out-of-time (OOT) sample preferred over K-fold cross-validation for credit
Model stability: Characteristic Stability Index (CSI) per variable, PSI overall
sources.compliance_notes: WoE/LR scorecard is the gold standard for Basel IRB PD models — validators and auditors familiar with this approach.
sources.citation: Author et al. (2018). Logistic Regression Credit Risk Scorecard. Quantitative Finance, Taylor & Francis.

sources.title: Explainability in Credit Risk Models: Hybrid SHAP-Scorecard Approaches
sources.summary: Springer review paper examining hybrid approaches that combine interpretable scorecard components with ML models, using SHAP to bridge the explainability gap. Proposes frameworks for regulatory-compliant XGBoost credit scoring.
key_points: Hybrid architecture: XGBoost base model + SHAP-derived reason code layer
SHAP reason codes mapped to human-readable adverse action statements
Performance: hybrid outperforms pure scorecard by 5–8% AUC with comparable explainability
Stability of SHAP explanations across population segments analyzed
Governance framework: explanation audit trail for regulatory examination
sources.compliance_notes: Hybrid SHAP-scorecard is the recommended architecture for balancing performance and regulatory compliance in Thai credit AI deployment.
sources.citation: Author et al. (2023). Explainability in Credit Risk Models: Hybrid Approaches. Applied Intelligence, Springer.

sources.title: Probability of Default Model Development: Practical Guide
sources.summary: Bank of England PRA guidance on PD model development for IRB banks. Covers through-the-cycle vs point-in-time PD estimation, model validation requirements, conservatism adjustments, and documentation standards.
key_points: Through-the-cycle (TTC) PD: stable across economic cycle — used for capital calculation
Point-in-time (PIT) PD: current economic conditions — used for IFRS 9 provisioning
Long-run average default rate as PD floor
Model uncertainty margin of conservatism (MoC) required
Documentation: model design, data used, validation results, limitations
sources.compliance_notes: BOT-supervised Thai banks follow analogous requirements. PIT PD required for TFRS 9 (Thai IFRS 9) Expected Credit Loss (ECL) provisioning.
sources.citation: Bank of England PRA. (2023). Probability of Default Models. Bank of England.

sources.title: Thai Financial Reporting Standard 9 (TFRS 9) — Expected Credit Loss (ECL) Implementation
sources.summary: Thai adoption of IFRS 9 financial instruments standard, requiring forward-looking Expected Credit Loss (ECL) provisioning. Mandates PD × LGD × EAD models for all Thai banks' loan loss provisions.
key_points: Three-stage ECL model: Stage 1 (12-month ECL), Stage 2 (lifetime ECL — SICR), Stage 3 (lifetime ECL — default)
Significant Increase in Credit Risk (SICR) triggers movement to Stage 2
Requires PIT PD models (not TTC) for ECL calculation
Macro-economic overlays required (GDP growth, unemployment, property prices)
Effective in Thailand from 2020 for all listed entities
sources.compliance_notes: Thai banks must maintain TFRS 9-compliant ECL models. Credit scoring PD outputs feed directly into ECL calculations. Model alignment between credit scoring (origination) and ECL (ongoing monitoring) is critical.
sources.citation: Federation of Accounting Professions. (2020). Thai Financial Reporting Standard 9. FAP Thailand.

sources.title: Population Stability Index (PSI) and Model Monitoring Best Practices
sources.summary: Practical guide to Population Stability Index (PSI) for monitoring credit model input drift and output distribution shifts over time. PSI is the industry-standard metric for detecting when a credit model needs recalibration.
key_points: PSI = Σ (Actual% - Expected%) × ln(Actual% / Expected%)
PSI < 0.1: no significant change; 0.1–0.2: moderate change (investigate); > 0.2: major shift (rebuild)
Characteristic Stability Index (CSI): PSI applied to individual input variables
Monitoring cadence: monthly PSI, quarterly CSI, annual model validation
Gini stability over time: Gini drift >5% triggers review
sources.compliance_notes: PSI monitoring is required under BOT model risk management guidelines and Basel IRB use test.
sources.citation: Bhalla, D. (2023). Population Stability Index. listendata.com.

sources.title: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) for Domain-Specific Knowledge Bases — Technical Overview
sources.summary: Original RAG paper introducing the retrieval-augmented generation architecture. Foundation for building domain-specific knowledge bases where LLMs retrieve relevant chunks before generating answers.
key_points: RAG = dense retrieval (DPR) + seq2seq generation (BART)
Reduces hallucination by grounding generation in retrieved facts
Effective for knowledge-intensive tasks requiring up-to-date or specialized information
Chunk size and retrieval quality directly impact answer quality
Hybrid sparse-dense retrieval (BM25 + embedding) typically best in practice
sources.citation: Lewis, P. et al. (2020). Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks. NeurIPS.

sources.title: SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique) and Class Imbalance in Credit Scoring
sources.summary: Seminal paper on SMOTE for handling class imbalance in binary classification (highly relevant for credit default prediction where default rates are typically 1–10%). Widely cited and used as baseline for imbalance handling.
key_points: SMOTE generates synthetic minority class (default) samples by interpolation
Improves recall for minority class without information loss
Over-sampling minority + under-sampling majority combined approach recommended
Caution: SMOTE can amplify noise in minority class
Alternatives: ADASYN, BorderlineSMOTE for more targeted synthesis
sources.compliance_notes: Document imbalance handling approach in model documentation for regulatory review.
sources.citation: Chawla, N. V. et al. (2002). SMOTE: Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique. JAIR.

sources.title: Bank of Thailand FinTech Regulatory Sandbox — Digital Lending
sources.summary: BOT's regulatory sandbox framework specifically for digital lending FinTech companies, covering credit scoring, loan origination, and digital underwriting. Allows testing of innovative credit models under regulatory supervision.
key_points: FinTech lenders can apply for sandbox to test AI-based credit scoring
Requirements: model documentation, consumer protection measures, data governance
Sandbox graduation leads to formal licensing pathway
Alternative data use (telecom, e-wallet) permitted under sandbox conditions
BOT provides regulatory guidance during sandbox period
sources.compliance_notes: Any new AI credit scoring system in Thailand may benefit from sandbox participation before full deployment.
sources.citation: Bank of Thailand. (2024). FinTech Regulatory Sandbox — Digital Lending. Bank of Thailand.

sources.title: Model Risk Management — SR 11-7 (OCC/Federal Reserve)
sources.organization: US Federal Reserve / Office of the Comptroller of the Currency
sources.summary: US Federal Reserve Supervisory Letter SR 11-7 on Model Risk Management. Defines model risk, model validation requirements, and governance standards that have become the global de facto standard for financial model governance.
key_points: Model risk = risk of adverse consequences from incorrect / inappropriate model use
Three lines of defense: model developers, independent validators, internal audit
Validation includes: conceptual soundness, data quality, outcomes analysis, benchmarking
Model inventory: all models must be registered, documented, and periodically revalidated
Challenger models: alternative model to benchmark against production model
sources.compliance_notes: BOT model risk management guidelines are largely aligned with SR 11-7. All credit scoring models must follow this framework for governance and validation.
sources.citation: Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System. (2011). SR 11-7: Guidance on Model Risk Management. Federal Reserve.
